content: Okay — Sam, 31, backend/ML engineer at a Paris logistics startup. Date 2026-03-05. Question aloud: should I learn Rust this year or go deeper in Python? Concretely: I use Python every day for backend APIs, data pipelines, and ML infra (training orchestration, nightly simulation jobs). I can realistically spare 6–8 hours/week on evenings/weekends. Career goal: reach Staff-level in 2–3 years and be useful both on ML and infra. Constraints: product needs short-term impact this year; we have one clear CPU-bound hotspot — the nightly route simulation that currently takes ~5 hours and costs ops time/compute. Hypothesis: a Rust rewrite could cut runtime significantly (target ≤1.5 hours), reduce infra cost, and give me systems credibility. Counter-hypothesis: deepening Python skills (Numba/Cython, async, better distributed tooling, advanced PyTorch/TF) might give equal or better ROI faster because I already ship in Python. Time question: to reach productive Rust competence maybe 3 months of focused work (around 96 hours if I do 8 hrs/week for 12 weeks). Opportunity cost: those 96 hours not spent on Python performance work or product features. Emotional factor: I enjoy lower-level work. Options I'm weighing: (A) 3-month Rust experiment this spring, prototype + benchmark + integrate if wins; (B) invest same time in Python performance techniques and algos; (C) a hybrid: learn just enough Rust to call a small native library from Python via FFI. Need to pick by mid-March so I can schedule. What am I missing, and what should I commit to?
What's your primary priority this year: immediate product impact (short-term ROI) or building a systems skillset for a 2–3 year career shift?

Both matter but ordered: 1) short-term impact at the startup this year, 2) diversify/level-up for 2–3 year horizon toward Staff. So any experiment must show measurable win within this year. Practical plan I like: a 3-month Rust experiment starting mid-March to mid-June — 8 hrs/week (≈96 hours). Build a prototype of the route simulator core in Rust, benchmark vs current Python (and a Numba/Cython optimized variant), and if Rust reduces runtime to ≤1.5 hours and the dev+maintenance cost is reasonable, integrate to production by Sep 30, 2026. If not, fallback to Python optimizations. That keeps short-term ROI guardrails. Is that a reasonable commitment?
What single concrete metric will decide the experiment a success (and what threshold)?

Success metric: end-to-end nightly simulation runtime reduced from ~5.0 hours to ≤1.5 hours (≥70% reduction) when swapping in the Rust component, measured on our staging cluster under representative load. Secondary metric: developer/maintenance overhead — if onboarding and future changes increase dev time >50% compared to current Python baseline, that’s a strike. Decision rule: if by 12 weeks we can't get ≥30% improvement in a stable prototype, stop the Rust path and spend remaining time on Python tooling. Timeline: start 2026-03-15, prototype complete 2026-06-15, production integration target 2026-09-30. Resources: The Rust Book + rustlings + one paid Udemy course, and 96 focused hours. Sound tight but actionable.
Do you have your manager’s buy-in to allocate ~10% of your work time and allow a production experiment with that timeline?

Yes — manager said they'll support if I present the ROI plan and weekly benchmarks; they’ll allow ~10% dev time for 3 months. Practical steps I'll take: 1) week 1–2 (2026-03-15 to 03-28): learn basics (The Rust Book, rustlings), set up repo and benchmarking harness; 2) weeks 3–8: implement core algorithm in Rust, iterate; 3) weeks 9–12: stress test, compare vs Cython/Numba version, write docs and integration adapter (gRPC/FFI). Deliverables: reproducible benchmarks, PR for a minimal service, cost estimate for production run. I'll budget 8 hrs/week, so 96 hours total. If prototype shows ≥70% runtime reduction and ops cost drop, schedule integration work for July–Sep.
If the Rust experiment doesn’t hit the threshold, what exactly will you do with the remaining effort/time?

Fallback plan: pivot to Python performance work — implement a Numba-accelerated version, explore Cython and pypy, profile for algorithmic improvements, and if needed hire an external contractor for a one-off C++/Rust native module. Decision thresholds: stop Rust path if <30% improvement by 6–8 weeks or if dev time to stabilize exceeds planned hours by >30%. Financially, I’ll compare compute savings vs development cost: target payback within 6–9 months after integration; otherwise it’s not worth it. Also, I’ll log weekly benchmarks and costs so the manager sees progress.
Will you commit to logging weekly benchmarks and a 6-week early-stop check so you can pivot quickly?

Yes — I’ll log weekly numbers (runtime, CPU hours, dev hours) and run the 6-week checkpoint on 2026-04-26: if no reproducible ≥30% improvement by then, I stop Rust work and switch to Python performance measures. Final decision: I’ll run the Rust experiment starting 2026-03-15, 8 hrs/week for 12 weeks, with the success threshold ≤1.5 hours nightly runtime and the fallbacks outlined. I feel good about this plan — it balances short-term ROI and long-term skill building. I’ll record results and re-evaluate by Sep 30, 2026.
